title: Sets
subtitle: Unordered collections that guarantee uniqueness
learning_objectives: Create a set and understand that it stores only unique values
Add and remove items with .add(), .remove(), and .discard()
Perform set operations: union, intersection, and difference
Use sets to remove duplicates from a list

sections.heading: What Is a Set?
sections.content:
A set is an unordered collection that stores only unique values — duplicates are automatically discarded. Sets are created with curly braces {} (but note that {} alone creates an empty dict, not a set — use set() for an empty set). Because sets are unordered, you cannot access elements by index.

Use .add(item) to add a value. Use .remove(item) to delete a value — it raises a KeyError if the item is not present. Use .discard(item) to silently do nothing if the item is missing.
sections.code_example:
# Creating a set
tags = {"python", "code", "python", "tutorial"}
print(tags)       # duplicate 'python' removed

# add and remove
tags.add("beginner")
print(tags)

tags.remove("tutorial")
print(tags)

# discard does not raise an error if missing
tags.discard("missing_tag")
print(len(tags))

# Empty set
empty = set()
print(type(empty))
sections.warning: Sets are unordered — when you print a set, the items may appear in a different order every time you run the code. That is normal and expected. Don't be surprised if your output order differs from the example.

sections.heading: Set Operations and Removing Duplicates
sections.content:
Sets support mathematical set operations with intuitive operators. | (union) combines both sets. & (intersection) returns only items in both sets. - (difference) returns items in the left set that are not in the right set.

A very practical use case: convert a list to a set and back to remove all duplicate values quickly: list(set(my_list)).
sections.code_example:
a = {1, 2, 3, 4}
b = {3, 4, 5, 6}

print(a | b)   # union
print(a & b)   # intersection
print(a - b)   # difference (in a but not b)

# Remove duplicates from a list
nums = [1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 4]
unique = list(set(nums))
print(unique)

exercises.title: Build a Set of Tags
exercises.prompt: Create a set called skills containing "python", "sql", and "excel". Add "python" again and add "tableau". Print the set and its length to verify no duplicates.
exercises.starter_code:
# Create a set called skills containing "python", "sql", and "excel"

# Add "python" again (it should not create a duplicate)
# Add "tableau"

# Print the set

# Print the length
hints: Create the set with {"python", "sql", "excel"} and then call .add() for the extra items
Adding 'python' a second time has no effect — the set already contains it

tests.message: Your code should run without any errors
The length should be 4 (python, sql, excel, tableau — no duplicates)

exercises.title: Deduplicate a List
exercises.prompt: Given votes = ["yes", "no", "yes", "yes", "no", "maybe"], convert it to a set to get the unique options, then convert it back to a list called options. Print options and its length.
exercises.starter_code:
votes = ["yes", "no", "yes", "yes", "no", "maybe"]

# Deduplicate using set and convert back to list

# Print the unique options

# Print the length
hints: set(votes) removes duplicates; wrap it in list() to convert back
The result should have 3 unique values: 'yes', 'no', 'maybe'

tests.message: Your code should run without any errors

quiz.question: What is the result of {1, 2, 3} & {2, 3, 4}?
quiz.explanation: The & operator returns the intersection — only elements present in both sets. 2 and 3 appear in both sets, so the result is {2, 3}.

quiz.question: What is the difference between .remove() and .discard() on a set?
options: remove() works on lists; discard() works on sets
remove() raises a KeyError if the item is missing; discard() does not
discard() returns the removed item; remove() returns None
quiz.explanation: Both methods delete an item from a set, but .remove() raises a KeyError if the item is not present, while .discard() silently does nothing. Use .discard() when you are not sure the item is in the set.

completion_message: Excellent! Sets are a surprisingly powerful tool — fast membership testing, automatic deduplication, and intuitive math operations. You have now covered lists, tuples, dicts, and sets!
